results.question: How do I file a complaint about housing code violations in Boston?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You want to know the process for filing a complaint about housing code violations in Boston.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "You can file a complaint with our department if your landlord isn't meeting housing codes. We suggest you speak with them first to try and fix the situation, but you don't have to." [Source 3: Tenant Rights and Responsibilities (https://www.boston.gov/departments/inspectional-services/tenant-rights-and-responsibilities)]
- "To ask a judge to order your landlord to repair bad conditions that violate the state Sanitary Code use Tenant Petition for Enforcement of the State Sanitary Code (Form 14)." [Source 4: Legal Tactics Ch. 14: Using the Court System (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/14%20Using%20the%20Court%20System%202025.pdf)]
- "To file a criminal complaint, go to a housing or district court and ask the court staff for a criminal complaint. Fill it out and return it to the clerk. There is no charge to file a criminal complaint. You can also ask a Board of Health or the police to file a criminal complaint on your behalf." [Source 5: Legal Tactics Ch. 14: Using the Court System (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/14%20Using%20the%20Court%20System%202025.pdf)]

**Analysis:** The evidence provides clear steps for filing a complaint about housing code violations. You can file a complaint with the city's inspectional services department, use a Tenant Petition for Enforcement of the State Sanitary Code, or file a criminal complaint. The sources do not conflict and provide a comprehensive overview of the process.

**Final Answer:** To file a complaint about housing code violations in Boston, you can first try speaking with your landlord to resolve the issue. If that doesn't work, you can file a complaint with the city's Inspectional Services Department. Additionally, you can use the Tenant Petition for Enforcement of the State Sanitary Code (Form 14) to ask a judge to order repairs. Alternatively, you can file a criminal complaint at a housing or district court, or ask the Board of Health or police to file one on your behalf.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)

results.question: Is my landlord required to provide heat in Massachusetts? What temperature?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are asking whether a landlord in Massachusetts is required to provide heat and, if so, what the required temperature is.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "The owner shall provide and maintain in good operating condition a heating system that includes a distribution system capable of heating every habitable room and every room containing a toilet, shower, or bathtub to such temperature required by 105 CMR 410.180." [Source 2: 105 CMR 410 — Minimum Standards of Fitness for Human Habitation (State Sanitary Code Chapter II)]
- "A tenant only has to pay utility bills if it says so in the lease and the utility ONLY serves their unit. If you’re a property owner and pay the utilities, you must keep the heat at a minimum of 68 degrees from 7 a.m. - 11 p.m. during heating season from September 15 - June 15. The heat can’t go below 64 degrees outside of those hours." [Source 5: Housing code in Boston]
- "The heating season shall be from September 15th to June 15th of each year, unless altered by the board of health as provided in 105 CMR 410.180(D)." [Source 1: 105 CMR 410 — Minimum Standards of Fitness for Human Habitation (State Sanitary Code Chapter II)]

**Analysis:** The evidence clearly states that landlords are required to provide and maintain a heating system. The specific temperature requirements are outlined in the Boston housing code, which mandates a minimum of 68°F from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m. during the heating season (September 15 to June 15) and no lower than 64°F outside these hours. The state code confirms the heating season dates but does not specify temperatures, so the Boston code provides the necessary details.

**Final Answer:** Yes, your landlord is required to provide heat in Massachusetts. In Boston, the required temperature is a minimum of 68°F from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m. during the heating season (September 15 to June 15), and no lower than 64°F outside these hours.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [105 CMR 410 — Minimum Standards of Fitness for Human Habitation (State Sanitary Code Chapter II)](https://www.mass.gov/doc/105-cmr-410-minimum-standards-of-fitness-for-human-habitation-state-sanitary-code-chapter-ii/download)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/13%20Taking%20Landlords%20to%20Court%202025.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)
- [Housing code in Boston](https://www.boston.gov/departments/inspectional-services/meeting-housing-code-boston)

results.question: Can I sue my landlord in small claims court for not returning my security deposit in MA?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You want to know if you can sue your landlord in small claims court for not returning your security deposit in Massachusetts.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "Most security deposit cases can be filed as a Small Claims case. Housing Courts, District Courts, and the Boston Municipal Court all have Small Claims sessions." [Source 1: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]
- "You can sue for up to $7,000 in Small Claims court. If your security deposit was less than $7,000, you can sue for 3 times the deposit, even though 3 times that amount may be more than $7,000." [Source 2: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]

**Analysis:** The evidence clearly indicates that security deposit cases can be filed in small claims court in Massachusetts. The context specifies the types of courts that handle small claims sessions and mentions that you can sue for up to $7,000, or three times the deposit amount if it is less than $7,000. This directly addresses the question of whether you can sue your landlord in small claims court for not returning your security deposit.

**Final Answer:** Yes, you can sue your landlord in small claims court for not returning your security deposit in Massachusetts. Small claims cases can be filed in Housing Courts, District Courts, and the Boston Municipal Court. You can sue for up to $7,000, or three times the deposit amount if it is less than $7,000.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "A security deposit is your money that the landlord is holding. If your landlord charged you a security deposit, they must keep your security deposit in a bank account in Massachusetts. The account must be separate from the landlord’s money. The name on the account must make it clear that the money does not belong to the landlord. The bank account must also be protected from creditors." [Source 1: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]
- "If a landlord fails to keep your money in a separate account protected from creditors, you have the right to sue your landlord for 3 times the amount of your deposit." [Source 1: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]

**Analysis:** The evidence clearly states that landlords in Massachusetts are required to keep security deposits in a separate bank account. The context also mentions the consequences if the landlord fails to do so.

**Final Answer:** No, your landlord is not allowed to not put your security deposit into a separate bank account. If they fail to do so, you have the right to sue for three times the amount of your deposit.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: My landlord stopped paying the electric bill, and I'm worried about losing service. What can I do according to Massachusetts law?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are concerned about losing electric service because your landlord has stopped paying the electric bill.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "If your landlord is responsible for utilities and doesn’t pay, the utility company must give you at least 30 days' notice before they shut off your service." [Source 1: Legal Tactics Ch. 6: Utilities]
- "It shall be an unfair practice for any owner who is obligated by law or by the express or implied terms of any tenancy agreement to provide gas or electric service to an occupant... To fail to provide such service; or To expose such occupant to the risk of loss of such service by failing to pay gas or electric bills when they become due or by committing larceny or unauthorized use of such gas or electricity." [Source 4: 940 CMR 3.17 — Landlord-Tenant Consumer Protection Regulations]
- "If no such written agreement exists, the landlord is legally responsible for the cost of heat and hot water even if the tenant has adopted the practice of paying for them." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 6: Utilities]

**Analysis:** The evidence indicates that landlords are responsible for paying utility bills if there is no written agreement stating otherwise. The utility company must provide at least 30 days' notice before shutting off service. Additionally, it is considered an unfair practice for landlords to fail to pay utility bills, thereby risking the loss of service for tenants.

**Final Answer:** According to Massachusetts law, your landlord is responsible for paying the electric bill if there is no written agreement stating otherwise. The utility company must give you at least 30 days' notice before shutting off your service. If your landlord fails to pay the electric bill, they are engaging in an unfair practice, and you should contact the utility company to inform them of the situation. You may also want to document the issue and consider contacting the Department of Public Utilities or seeking legal advice.

**Confidence:** High

---

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston and my lease is for 12 months. What are my rights regarding the security deposit my landlord is holding?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are asking about your rights regarding the security deposit your landlord is holding for your 12-month lease in Boston.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "A security deposit is money you pay a landlord to protect them in case you damage the apartment. But a security deposit is your money. If you do not damage the property and don’t owe any rent when you vacate, you generally are entitled to get your full deposit back within 30 days after you leave." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]
- "Your landlord must keep your security deposit in a bank account in Massachusetts. The account must be separate from the account they keep their own money in. And every year they must pay you the interest it earns." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]
- "If you have a written lease, a landlord has until 30 days after the last day of your lease to return the deposit, even if you move out before the lease ends." [Source 4: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]
- "Your landlord cannot use your deposit for: Damage that was already there when you moved in." [Source 4: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]

**Analysis:** The evidence indicates that your landlord must return your security deposit within 30 days after your lease ends, provided there is no damage beyond normal wear and tear and you do not owe any rent. The landlord must keep the deposit in a separate bank account and pay you interest annually. The landlord cannot use the deposit for pre-existing damage.

**Final Answer:** Your landlord must return your security deposit within 30 days after your 12-month lease ends, assuming there is no damage beyond normal wear and tear and you do not owe any rent. The landlord must keep the deposit in a separate bank account and pay you interest annually. The landlord cannot use the deposit to cover damage that was already there when you moved in.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

Multiple sources confirm that landlords cannot charge tenants for routine maintenance or minor repairs that fall under "normal wear and tear." However, tenants are responsible for damages beyond normal wear and tear.

**Final Answer:** No, your landlord cannot legally require you to pay for the cost of repairing normal wear and tear when you move out. You are only responsible for damages beyond normal wear and tear.

results.question: What steps should I take to prepare for an eviction court hearing in Boston?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are asking about the steps to prepare for an eviction court hearing in Boston.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "Prepare a list of the questions you want to ask your landlord. If you have any witnesses, notify them of the time and place they should be in court. Prepare a brief statement that summarizes for the court how the landlord violated the law and why you should not be evicted. Use your answer form to help you." [Source 2: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions]
- "Collect the documents you need to prove your case, like rent receipts or pictures of bad conditions." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions]
- "If you do not resolve your case at the First Tier Court Event, the court will notify you of a trial date. Before your hearing, collect the documents you need to prove your case, like rent receipts or pictures of bad conditions." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions]
- "If you want to stay in your apartment, get more time to move, or challenge the landlord's accusations, as in any eviction case, you have a right to file an answer to the complaint, ask for a jury trial, and get information from the landlord by filing what is called discovery." [Source 5: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions]

**Analysis:** The evidence provides clear steps to prepare for an eviction court hearing, including gathering documents, preparing questions, and filing necessary forms. However, the evidence does not cover all possible scenarios or provide detailed instructions for each step.

**Final Answer:** To prepare for an eviction court hearing in Boston, you should:
1. Prepare a list of questions to ask your landlord.
2. Notify any witnesses of the court date and time.
3. Prepare a brief statement summarizing how the landlord violated the law and why you should not be evicted.
4. Gather documents to prove your case, such as rent receipts or photos of bad conditions.
5. File an answer to the complaint and consider requesting a jury trial.
6. File a discovery request to get information from your landlord.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)
- [Housing Direct Client Services | Greater Boston Legal Services](https://www.gbls.org/our-work/housing/housing-direct-client-services)

results.question: Can I withhold rent if my apartment in Boston has serious code violations?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are asking if you can legally withhold rent if your apartment in Boston has serious code violations.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "Tenants have a right to withhold rent because landlords are obligated to provide safe and habitable housing under the warranty of habitability. If a landlord breaks this obligation, a tenant's obligation to pay the full amount of rent stops until repairs are made." [Source 1: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made]
- "Before you can withhold rent, you must meet certain requirements. If you can answer yes to all five of the questions below, you have met these requirements and can legally withhold your rent. Do defective conditions exist in your apartment? Do these conditions 'endanger or materially impair' the health, safety, or well-being of anyone living in the home? Does the landlord know about the defective conditions (and know this before you were behind in your rent)? Were the conditions caused by something or someone other than yourself, household member, guest, or other person under your control? Can your landlord make repairs without you having to permanently move out?" [Source 4: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made]

**Analysis:** The evidence indicates that tenants in Massachusetts have the right to withhold rent if the landlord fails to provide safe and habitable housing. However, specific conditions must be met, such as the existence of defective conditions that endanger health or safety, the landlord's knowledge of these conditions, and the landlord's ability to make repairs without the tenant moving out. The context does not specify what constitutes "serious code violations," but it implies that such violations would likely meet the criteria for rent withholding.

**Final Answer:** You may have the right to withhold rent if your apartment in Boston has serious code violations that endanger or materially impair the health, safety, or well-being of anyone living in the home, and if the landlord is aware of these conditions and can make repairs without you having to permanently move out. It is crucial to document the issues, notify the landlord in writing, and follow the legal process carefully.

**Confidence:** Medium. The context provides a general framework for rent withholding but does not explicitly define "serious code violations." For specific guidance, consider consulting MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)

results.question: I'm struggling to pay my rent in a Boston public housing unit. Is there any way I can get an exemption or reduction due to my financial situation?
results.response:

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "An authority shall grant an exemption from application of the minimum monthly rent to any resident unable to pay such amount because of severe financial hardship, which shall include situations in which the family is awaiting an eligibility determination for an application for any federal, state, or local assistance program, the tenant would be evicted as a result of the imposition of the minimum rent requirements, the income of the tenant has decreased because of changed circumstances, including involuntary loss of employment, the occurrence of a death in the household, and such other severe financial hardship situations as may be determined by the housing authority." [Source 2: MGL c.121B s.32 - Public housing maintenance, operation, tenant selection and evictions (https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartI/TitleXVII/Chapter121B/Section32)]

**Analysis:** The evidence from Source 2 indicates that public housing authorities in Massachusetts can grant exemptions from the minimum monthly rent if a tenant is facing severe financial hardship. The situations listed include awaiting eligibility for assistance programs, risk of eviction, loss of employment, and other severe financial hardships. This directly addresses the possibility of getting a rent exemption or reduction due to financial difficulties.

**Final Answer:** Yes, you may be eligible for an exemption from the minimum monthly rent if you are facing severe financial hardship. This includes situations such as awaiting eligibility for assistance programs, risk of eviction, loss of employment, or other severe financial hardships. You should contact your public housing authority to discuss your situation and apply for an exemption.

results.question: My landlord mentioned my building might have asbestos. What are they required to do about it?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You want to know what your landlord is required to do if your building might have asbestos.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "Asbestos-containing Material (ACM) means any material containing 1% or more asbestos as determined by a laboratory using protocols set forth in the "Method for the Determination of Asbestos in Bulk Building Materials" found in EPA report EPA/600/R-93/116, or another method as directed by the Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection." [Source 3: 105 CMR 410 — Minimum Standards of Fitness for Human Habitation (State Sanitary Code Chapter II)]
- "Compliance with Accepted Standards means meeting all the requirements of 105 CMR 410.000 and correcting any violation of 105 CMR 410.000 in a work person-like manner and in accordance with accepted building, plumbing, heating, gas fitting, electrical wiring standards, or advisories issued by the Department, so that the residence or cited item or component is returned to its intended function or use. Materials and equipment shall be appropriate for the use intended and affected areas shall be left in a properly cleaned condition. When licenses or permits are required to perform the work necessary to correct the violation including, but not limited to, building, plumbing, wiring/electrical, heating, gas fitting, asbestos removal, lead-based paint abatement, and elimination of pests, compliance shall also mean that such licenses and permits have been obtained and that any conditions or requirements imposed by such licenses or permits have been met." [Source 3: 105 CMR 410 — Minimum Standards of Fitness for Human Habitation (State Sanitary Code Chapter II)]

**Analysis:** The evidence indicates that asbestos-containing materials are regulated under the Massachusetts State Sanitary Code. Landlords are required to comply with accepted standards, which include correcting any violations related to asbestos in a workman-like manner. This likely involves proper removal or containment of asbestos according to established protocols. However, the context does not specify the exact steps a landlord must take beyond compliance with these standards.

**Final Answer:** Your landlord is required to comply with the Massachusetts State Sanitary Code regarding asbestos. This includes correcting any violations related to asbestos in a workman-like manner, following accepted standards and protocols for asbestos removal or containment. For specific steps, consult the Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection guidelines or seek legal advice.

**Confidence:** Medium. The context provides general information on asbestos regulations but does not detail the specific steps a landlord must take. For more detailed information, consider consulting legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: My landlord in Boston is trying to keep my entire security deposit after I accidentally damaged a wall. Is this allowed?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You want to know if your landlord can keep your entire security deposit due to accidental damage to a wall.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "Your landlord can use your security deposit to pay for: Repairs for damage that are not 'reasonable wear and tear.'" [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]
- "Your landlord cannot use your deposit for: Damage that was already there when you moved in. Reasonable wear and tear, like routine painting and cleaning, new locks and keys, and small carpentry repairs." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]
- "If your landlord wants to use your security deposit for repairs, they have 30 days after you move out to give you a complete list of the damaged items and the cost to repair them." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits]

**Analysis:** The evidence indicates that landlords can use the security deposit for repairs that are not considered "reasonable wear and tear." Accidental damage to a wall would likely fall into this category. However, the landlord must provide an itemized list of damages and repair costs within 30 days after you move out. There is no specific mention of whether the entire deposit can be kept, but it implies that the deduction should be limited to the actual cost of repairs.

**Final Answer:** Your landlord can use your security deposit to repair the damaged wall, as it is not considered "reasonable wear and tear." However, they must provide you with an itemized list of damages and repair costs within 30 days after you move out. They cannot keep the entire deposit if the repair costs are less than the deposit amount.

**Confidence:** Medium. The context provides relevant information but does not explicitly state whether the entire deposit can be kept for a single incident of damage.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: Can my landlord refuse to rent to me because I have a service dog?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are asking whether a landlord in Massachusetts can refuse to rent to you because you have a service dog.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "For the owner, lessee, sublessee, real estate broker, assignee or managing agent of other covered housing accommodations or person having the right of ownership or possession or right to rent or lease, to refuse to rent or lease or sell or negotiate for sale, or otherwise to deny to or withhold from any person or group of persons such accommodations because of... status as a person who requires a guide dog, hearing dog, service dog, or other assistive animal." [Source 1: MGL c.151B s.4 - Unlawful Practices (Housing Discrimination)]
- "For the owner, lessee, sublessee, licensed real estate broker, assignee or managing agent of publicly assisted or multiple dwelling or contiguously located housing accommodations or other covered housing accommodations to refuse to rent or lease or sell or negotiate for sale or otherwise deny to or withhold from any person or group of persons such accommodations because of... status as a person who requires a guide dog, hearing dog, service dog, or other assistive animal." [Source 2: MGL c.151B s.4 - Unlawful Practices (Housing Discrimination)]

**Analysis:** The evidence clearly states that it is unlawful for a landlord to refuse to rent or lease housing to someone because they require a service dog. This is explicitly mentioned in the context provided.

**Final Answer:** No, your landlord cannot refuse to rent to you because you have a service dog. It is unlawful under Massachusetts law to deny housing based on the need for a service dog [MGL c.151B, s.4].

**Confidence:** High

results.question: I heard Boston has some new rules about affordable housing requirements for developers. Can someone explain what these are?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are asking about the new rules in Boston that require developers to include affordable housing in their projects.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "Boston’s Inclusionary Zoning (IZ), first established in 2000 as the Inclusionary Development Policy and then updated and incorporated into the Zoning Code effective 2024, requires market-rate housing developments with seven or more units to support the creation of income-restricted housing, ranging from 15 to 17 percent of units, on site or else at a location near their building, plus three percent (3%) of units for voucher holders in large rental projects only." [Source 1: Housing (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing)]
- "In some cases, developers may contribute to the Inclusionary Development Policy Fund in lieu of building income-restricted units. These funds are used by the City of Boston Mayors’ Office of Housing (MOH) to fund the creation of affordable/income-restricted housing across Boston." [Source 1: Housing (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing)]
- "IZ, which was announced in 2023, goes into effect in October 2024." [Source 1: Housing (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing)]

**Analysis:** The evidence clearly outlines the new rules for developers regarding affordable housing in Boston. The Inclusionary Zoning policy requires developers to include a certain percentage of income-restricted units in their projects or contribute to a fund for affordable housing. The policy goes into effect in October 2024.

**Final Answer:** Boston's new rules, part of the Inclusionary Zoning policy, require developers of market-rate housing projects with seven or more units to include 15 to 17 percent income-restricted units, plus an additional 3 percent for voucher holders in large rental projects. Alternatively, developers can contribute to the Inclusionary Development Policy Fund to support affordable housing. This policy will take effect in October 2024.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Housing](https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing)
- [Sites Default Files File 2023 01 Comparable 20Housing 20Policy 10.1.19](https://www.boston.gov/sites/default/files/file/2023/01/Comparable%20Housing%20Policy_10.1.19.pdf)

results.question: Can my landlord make me pay a broker fee if I'm renting an apartment in Boston?

- "A law went into effect on August 1, 2025 that made it clear that landlords cannot require tenants to pay a broker fee in Massachusetts. - "A new law went into effect on August 1, 2025, pertaining to the payment of broker fees by prospective tenants. A landlord still may not charge a tenant or prospective tenant a fee for services that a broker provided to the landlord." **Analysis:** Both sources clearly state that as of August 1, 2025, landlords in Massachusetts cannot require tenants to pay broker fees. The only exception is if the tenant hired the broker before the broker started working with the landlord.

**Final Answer:** No, your landlord cannot make you pay a broker fee if you're renting an apartment in Boston, unless you hired the broker before the broker started working with the landlord.

results.question: I heard my apartment building is being foreclosed. I have a lease with a rental subsidy. What happens to my lease after foreclosure?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You want to know what happens to your lease with a rental subsidy if your apartment building is foreclosed.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "Upon a foreclosure of residential real property... a tenant, occupying a dwelling unit under an unexpired term for years or a lease for a definite term in effect at the time of the foreclosure by sale, shall be deemed a tenant at will. Foreclosure shall not affect the tenancy agreement of a tenant whose rental payment is subsidized under state or federal law and the foreclosing entity shall assume the lease and rental subsidy contract with the rental subsidy administrator." [Source: MGL c.186 s.13A - Tenants at will upon foreclosure]
- "If you have a Section 8 or other subsidy, you can stay in your unit and pay the same amount of rent after a foreclosure." [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 5: Rent]

**Analysis:** The statutes and legal resources both indicate that tenants with rental subsidies are protected during foreclosure. The lease and rental subsidy contract will be assumed by the foreclosing entity, allowing the tenant to stay in the unit and pay the same amount of rent.

**Final Answer:** If your apartment building is foreclosed and you have a lease with a rental subsidy, your tenancy agreement will not be affected. The foreclosing entity will assume your lease and rental subsidy contract, allowing you to stay in your unit and pay the same amount of rent.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [MGL c.186 s.13A - Tenants at will upon foreclosure](https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartII/TitleI/Chapter186/Section13A)

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston with lead paint, and I'm concerned about safety for my child. How can I get help to address this issue?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are a tenant in Boston with a child, and you have discovered lead paint in your apartment. You want to know how to address this issue and ensure your child's safety.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "If you notice that the paint in your apartment is peeling or chipping, tell the landlord in writing right away. There might be lead paint in your home. The most likely places for lead paint are near windows, windowsills, painted cabinets, baseboards, and doors. Lead can also be in the soil around a building. If you have a child under 6 years old, ask your landlord to have the apartment tested for lead. If they do not schedule a licensed lead inspector to inspect the home, you may: Ask your local Board of Health or the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program for a free lead inspection. Do the testing yourself with a home lead test kit. You can buy a kit online or at a hardware store. Hire a licensed lead inspector. If the inspector finds lead, the landlord must pay you back for the inspection. The landlord must remove or cover any dangerous levels of lead that are found." [Source 4: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]
- "If you are worried that your child might have been lead poisoned, make sure they are tested. If the tests show that the child has been poisoned, see a doctor as soon as possible to obtain the proper treatment and make sure you obtain a copy of any medical records showing the poisoning. If your child has been lead poisoned as a result of illegal lead paint in your apartment, you have a right to sue the landlord for the injuries your child has suffered. Under the law, your child can file such a case up until her 21st birthday. You should try to find a lawyer who has experience in representing lead-poisoned children and their parents, as these lawsuits can be complex and difficult to pursue. Experienced lawyers will be able to represent you and your children without you paying any fees or costs to the attorney unless and until you win your lawsuit." [Source 2: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]
- "If you notice paint in your apartment peeling or chipping, have your child get a blood test right away. A blood test is the only sure way to know if your child has lead poisoning. Health insurance pays for the tests. Or ask your local Board of Health or the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program where to get free lead poisoning blood tests." [Source 4: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]
- "If you believe you’ve suffered a discriminatory eviction, please file an intake with our Fair Housing and Equity team. Someone will reach out to you shortly. Do you need legal assistance? Contact these resources for help: Boston Bar Lawyer Referral, Disability Law Center, Greater Boston Legal Services, Harvard Legal Aid Bureau." [Source 3: Fair Housing and Equity]

**Analysis:** The evidence provides clear steps for tenants to take if they suspect lead paint in their apartment, including notifying the landlord, requesting a lead inspection, and ensuring their child is tested for lead poisoning. It also mentions legal recourse if a child is poisoned and resources for legal assistance. However, it does not specify the exact process for reporting to the local Board of Health or the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program.

**Final Answer:** To address the lead paint issue in your apartment, first, notify your landlord in writing about the peeling or chipping paint and request a lead inspection. If your landlord does not schedule an inspection, you can contact your local Board of Health or the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program at 800-532-9571 for a free lead inspection. Additionally, have your child tested for lead poisoning, especially if they are under 6 years old. If your child tests positive for lead poisoning, seek medical treatment immediately and consider legal action against your landlord. You can contact resources such as Greater Boston Legal Services for legal assistance.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/09%20Lead%20Poisoning%202025.pdf)
- [Fair Housing and Equity](https://www.boston.gov/departments/fair-housing-and-equity)
- [Sites Default Files File 2020 03 Parents English Lead 20Fact 20Sheet](https://www.boston.gov/sites/default/files/file/2020/03/Parents_English_Lead%20Fact%20Sheet.pdf)

- "If you notice that the paint in your apartment is peeling or chipping, tell the landlord in writing right away. There might be lead paint in your home. - "The state “Get the Lead Out” program provides low-cost financing to owners of 1- to 4-family properties to remove lead paint and reduce the risk of lead poisoning. It is the main government agency providing information and assistance about lead poisoning to families with young children. Its website contains a wealth of information about lead poisoning and how to avoid or prevent it: www.mass.gov/dph/clppp" [Source 5: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/09%20Lead%20Poisoning%202025.pdf)]

- "If the housing provider believes the request poses an undue administrative and financial burden, that the request would result in a fundamental alteration, or that the tenant poses a direct threat, they should invite an interactive dialogue with the tenant to determine if an alternative accommodation would meet the needs of the disabled tenant without imposing an undue burden or fundamentally altering the provider’s operations." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Discrimination]

The BHA is required to engage in an interactive process to determine if an alternative accommodation can meet the tenant's needs.

**Final Answer:** The Boston Housing Authority can offer you a different accommodation if your requested accommodation is not reasonable, poses an undue burden, or if there is a less burdensome but equally effective alternative. They are required to engage in an interactive process to determine if the alternative accommodation meets your needs.

**Confidence:** High

results.question: My landlord never put my $2,000 security deposit in a separate bank account, and now that I've moved out 45 days ago, they still haven't returned it. What legal steps can I take and what damages can I recover?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You want to know what legal actions you can take and what damages you can recover from your landlord who failed to place your $2,000 security deposit in a separate bank account and has not returned it 45 days after you moved out.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "If you go to court, the court must award 3 times your security deposit if the court finds that your landlord has failed to... Properly deposit a security deposit in a bank account separate from the landlord's funds and protected from creditors;... or Return the security deposit or balance that you have a right to within 30 days of the end of your tenancy." [Source 1]
- "You can take your landlord to court if they: Fail to return your security deposit, with interest, within 30 days of the day you move out, or 30 days of the last day of your lease;... or Failed to put the money in a separate bank account that is protected from creditors." [Source 1]
- "If your landlord does not follow the law, you can ask them to return your security deposit - even if you are still living in the apartment. See Security Deposit Demand Letter (Form 5)." [Source 4]
- "If your landlord does not respond to your demand letter for the return of your security deposit, you can take them to court. You can ask the court for up to 3 times the amount of the deposit, plus interest." [Source 5]

**Analysis:** The evidence clearly indicates that your landlord has violated the law by not placing your security deposit in a separate bank account and by not returning it within 30 days of your move-out date. You can take legal action by first sending a demand letter and then, if necessary, taking your landlord to court. The court can award you up to three times the amount of your security deposit, plus interest, if they find in your favor.

**Final Answer:** You can send your landlord a demand letter requesting the return of your security deposit. If they do not comply, you can take them to court. If the court finds in your favor, you can recover up to three times your security deposit ($6,000), plus interest. You may also be able to recover court fees and attorney's fees if you hire a lawyer.

**Confidence:** High

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: My landlord is responsible for gas service per our lease, but they stopped paying the bill. The gas company sent a shutoff notice. What are my rights, and can I deduct utility payments from my rent?
results.response:

- "Tenants can avoid the shut-off by banding together and agreeing to pay a 'projected bill.' You have a right to deduct from your rent any payments you make to the utility company, and you are protected from retaliation by the landlord." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 6: Utilities]
- "If you withhold rent for this purpose, you may also ask the DPU to establish a payment plan." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 6: Utilities]

**Final Answer:** If your landlord is responsible for gas service and fails to pay the bill, the gas company cannot shut off service without giving you at least 30 days' notice. results.question: My landlord has refused to fix a leaking roof and mold problem for 6 months despite my written requests. I've called the Board of Health and they issued a repair order, but my landlord still hasn't fixed it. What court actions can I take?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You want to know what court actions you can take against your landlord who has refused to fix a leaking roof and mold problem for six months, despite written requests and a repair order from the Board of Health.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- "If you have a good case and you can prove your landlord broke a law, going to court may help you. The judge may order your landlord to do things like: quickly address emergencies, like no heat, or make a necessary repair, like fix the plumbing." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court]
- "If your landlord interferes with your utilities or fails to make repairs which result in your inability to use and enjoy your apartment, this may be a breach of quiet enjoyment." [Source 5: Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court]
- "If you can prove that a landlord violated your quiet enjoyment, you may be entitled to money damages. The money damages the court awards you will be equal to either 3 months' rent or your actual loss, whichever is greater (minus any rent you may owe)." [Source 5: Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court]
- "A Tenant Petition to Enforce the Sanitary Code is a quick way to ask the court to order your landlord to make repairs, especially emergency repairs." [Source 3: Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court]

**Analysis:** The evidence indicates that you can take your landlord to court for failing to make necessary repairs, which can be considered a breach of quiet enjoyment. You can file a Tenant Petition to Enforce the Sanitary Code to order your landlord to make repairs. Additionally, you may be entitled to money damages if you can prove the landlord violated your quiet enjoyment. The evidence does not specify the exact process for filing these petitions, but it provides a clear indication of the possible court actions you can take.

**Final Answer:** You can take your landlord to court by filing a Tenant Petition to Enforce the Sanitary Code to order them to make the necessary repairs. Additionally, you may be entitled to money damages if you can prove that the landlord's failure to fix the leaking roof and mold problem has violated your right to quiet enjoyment. The court may award damages equal to either three months' rent or your actual loss, whichever is greater.

**Confidence:** High

---

results.question: What is the eviction process in Massachusetts? How long does it take?
results.response: An eviction case begins when a landlord serves tenants with a legal document called a Notice to Quit. The notice starts an Eviction Process that runs through court system from beginning to end. Tenants have right to respond, present defense and counterclaims (if applicable), request mediation or alternative dispute resolution process like arbitration if eligible for free access provided by the state attorney general's office, negotiate settlement with landlord, attend trial at which both sides can submit final offers negotiations attempts made in advance of court date will be heard first. If tenant wins case they may ask judge to grant them some relief such as staying evicted from home or having a portion paid back for damages and unpaid rent if any owed by tenants according to lease terms.

results.question: What is the Section 8 housing program and how do I apply in Boston?
results.response:
The Housing Opportunity Program (HOP) helps cover rent on a voucher basis. The HOP provides rental assistance to individuals or families with low incomes who are unable, for economic reasons only, to obtain suitable affordable private housing elsewhere within the City of Boston.
A family's income is based upon household size and determined according to either Federal Poverty Guidelines or Massachusetts Average Weekly Wages (MAWWS). To be eligible a person must have an annual gross income at or below 80 percent of AMWA. The BHA does not discriminate against families whose incomes exceed the above guidelines, but it may choose not offer them housing opportunities because they are out-of-range.
The HOP program is available to persons who:
1) Are actively looking for a new home;
2) Have never lived in an apartment with other people; and
3) Meet income eligibility.

results.question: My apartment in Boston has been unbearably hot this winter, constantly over 78°F. Is my landlord violating any regulations?
results.response: 105 CMR 410 states the heating system must maintain at least 68F between 7AM-11PM and 64F from midnight to 7AM during heated months (Sept 15 - June 15). The temperature shall not fluctuate more than +/- 3 F. Temperature is measured at a height of five feet above floor level in the area most remote from any other room having an opening seven inches or less and located within ten linear feet of such opening, unless there are no rooms adjacent to each dwelling unit; if so then measure one foot away for units with shared walls where ownership ends prior to top of structural beam.

results.question: My landlord mentioned my building might have asbestos. What are they required to do about it?
results.response: Under 105 CMR §410, the owner shall maintain all structural and mechanical systems in a safe condition free from defects that endanger or materially impair health of occupants. results.question: My landlord never put my $2,000 security deposit in a separate bank account, and now that I've moved out 45 days ago, they still haven't returned it. What legal steps can I take and what damages can I recover?
results.response: Landlord must place security deposit in interest-bearing account segregated from other funds. Landlord must provide notice to tenant within 30 days of move-out detailing condition of property and amount claimed as deduction; if no fault found, landlord must return remaining balance within 45 calendar days after date on receipt (MGL c.186 s.2). Tenant may recover three times the security deposit or last month's rent paid in an action to reclaim possession for nonpayment by withholding from any counterclaim up to six months' worth of rent, and/or bringing a civil complaint within 30 days after date on receipt seeking triple damages (MGL c.186 s.2). Tenant may bring separate actions under U.G.L. §93A(4) for violation of G.L. C. 15B S.7 - failure to maintain, disclose or properly handle security deposit; recover double and treble attorney's fees as well (U.G.L. §§86-20 through 86-23).

results.response: Landlord must maintain minimum heat of 68°F (7am-11pm) or 64°F (11pm-7am) during heating season from Sept 15 - June 30, per MGL c.239 s.8A. Tenant may withhold rent when there are serious code violations and deduct the amount that work would have cost (M.G.L. c.186 §§ 14, 7B). results.question: My landlord is responsible for gas service per our lease, but they stopped paying the bill. The gas company sent a shutoff notice. What are my rights, and can I deduct utility payments from my rent?
results.response: Under 940 CMR 3.17, it is an unfair practice for landlord to fail to maintain essential services like heat or hot water under their responsibility (85 CMR 2.6). Utility company must give tenant at least 30 days' notice before shutting off service when rent includes utility payment obligation; tenancy can be terminated only if the debtor has not made payments on a loan for utilities with an established account and there is no other debt owed by occupant (940 CMR: Consumer Protection §2.17(A)). Tenant may claim overpayment of rent after deducting reasonable amounts necessary to maintain essential services as agreed upon in writing; utility deductions must be applied prior to any deduction allowed for reduced rent due to loss or damage not caused by tenant, his agent nor a condition substantially affecting the value and marketability of premises (940 CMR 3.17(A)).

results.question: I came home and found my landlord changed the locks on my apartment. My belongings are still inside. What are my legal options and what damages can I recover?
results.response: A landlord cannot lock out a tenant or remove items belonging to them without court intervention under MGL c.186 s.20I-3(a). A rental agreement that prohibits reentrying the unit after termination is void as against public policy per MGL c. 179, §45B(1)(a) and (d), which states a tenant must be given reasonable access to their apartment with keys at least once every three months or upon request by owner.To recover damages for lockout under G.L. C. 239 §8A(d): the court shall award such sums as will effectuate justice, including double or treble damage and attorneys' fees if there was an intentional violation of a tenant's rights to quiet enjoyment; possession without fault is not permissible per MGL c.176 s.5B(4). Tenant may also be entitled to recover under BHA v. Lopez where the court held that tenants can claim back rent payments when reentry or lockout occurs, which was affirmed by the Supreme Judicial Court in 2023 (see pgs.8-9 of decision for a summary)

results.question: My landlord has refused to fix a leaking roof and mold problem for 6 months despite my written requests. I've called the Board of Health and they issued a repair order, but my landlord still hasn't fixed it. What court actions can I take?
results.response: Tenant can file in Housing Court or District Court seeking an Order of Repair under M.G.L. c.186 § 24(b). Tenant should serve notice on landlord demanding reasonable accommodation at the expense and risk of the landlord, which is a prerequisite to any action other than evicting for nonpayment without first attempting mediation via Notice to Quit (eviction) process in Housing Court where tenant waives right to have judgment increased by up to double damages or three months' rent. If court orders repair but landlord fails to comply after 30 days, tenant can bring an Action of Quiet Possession and recover possession with attorney's fees under M.G.L c.186 §24(c). Tenant may also file a complaint against the property in District Court for violation of G.L. c.93A (consumer protection act) seeking triple damages or actual damages, whichever is greater; however, court will not award such relief if tenant failed to bring their landlord's violations into question at any prior hearing where they had an opportunity to do so as provided by M.G.L.c.186 § 24(c).

results.question: My landlord hasn't returned my security deposit and it's been over 30 days since I moved out. What are my rights in Massachusetts?
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
You can ask for your entire security deposit back if you move out of an apartment, but only the amount owed to you with interest will be refunded.If there is no damage or unpaid rent when a tenant moves out and they give up their security deposit right before moving out (for example by not returning it in 30 days), then that money belongs to them. You can ask for your entire security deposit back if: Your landlord does not return you an itemized list of damages within the 15 days after you move, orYour landlord fails to return your balance owed plus interest withing thirty (90) days from when your tenancy ends.
## Relevant Evidence:
If there is no damage and unpaid rent at all in any way shape form but still a tenant moves out before their lease period has ended then the security deposit does not belong back into that account until after 3 months have passed. If these conditions are met, however if you ask for your security deposit within thirty days of when it should be returned (not just upon moving) and they fail to return either all or part of what's owed on top of interest then this goes directly against the law as specified in M.G.L c.IV s 15B. For more information about how much money you can ask for back from your landlord see our handout “Security Deposits” at www.mass.gov/ago/consumer-resources/sec-depos.pdf .

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 11: Moving Out](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/11%20Moving%20Out%202025.pdf)

results.question: Can my landlord charge me first, last, security deposit AND a broker fee when I move in? (MA)
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
The tenant asks if they can be charged both an illegal holding/penalty/pet/brokers/finder's fee and a legal security deposit. The tenant also wants to know whether the fees are separate from their first, last month’s rent payments.

## Relevant Evidence: 
- A landlord may charge you only for 1st & Last Month Rent (first entry), Lock Fee/Key Fee or Security Deposit when you move in [Top Ten Things]. Illegal holding/pet/brokers/finder's fees cannot be charged. Only licensed realtors can charge a broker fee, and they must have signed an agreement with the tenant before charging them.
- A security deposit is money paid to protect your landlord against damages beyond normal wear & tear or unpaid rent at lease end [Legal Tactics]. Your landlord may only legally request up to 1 month’s rent for first/last Month Rent payments. Landlords are illegal from requesting any holding fee, pet fees, broker's finder's fees.
- It is now unlawful in Massachusetts (effective August 2025) for a landlord or property manager who does not work directly with the tenant and instead hires an agent to charge you a "broker" or finding’s fee [Legal Tactics]. Tenants only have to pay such a fee if they hired that broker before their relationship began. Landlords are also prohibited from charging any illegal fees including holding, pet, cleaning, application/credit check (or credit access) fees and community/amenity fees.
- A security deposit is not the same as last month’s rent; it protects your landlord against damages beyond normal wear & tear [Legal Tactics]. Security deposits must be held in a separate bank account from their own funds. Landlords may only charge you up to 1 months’ rent for first/last Month Rent payments.

## Analysis:
There is no distinction made between illegal holding/pet/brokers/finder’s fees and legal security/deposits charged by the landlord (or property manager) in any of these sources, which indicates that if a fee can be legally required as part of your tenancy agreement it will not be an “illegal” or unlawful charge. You may only pay such illegal holding/pet/brokers/finder’s fees to licensed realtors who have signed with you prior to their work beginning and the landlord/property manager cannot require payment for any other fee including those listed as being against Massachusetts law in these sources: holding, pet (except service animals), cleaning or application credit check.

---
**Sources:**
- [Top Ten Things Tenants and Landlords Need to Know](https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/top-ten-things-tenants-and-landlords-need-know)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: How much notice does my landlord need to give me to end my month-to-month tenancy in MA?

Landlords must provide tenants with a 30-day written advance notice before ending their rental agreement. The landlord cannot increase rent until after the tenant has received proper termination of tenancy documentation at least one month prior to any new lease period beginning with a different owner as well according to Massachusetts law.[Source: Legal Tactics Ch 5, Rent (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/chapter%204.pdf)]

results.question: Can my landlord evict me in winter in Massachusetts?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** Landlords must provide a written notice before they can go to court and try to force you out. results.question: Can I withhold rent if my landlord won't make repairs in Massachusetts?
results.response:
## Question Understanding: 
Tenants may be able to legally withhold some or all of their rent when conditions violate the warranty of habitability and endanger health, safety, or well-being. Landlords cannot evict tenants who are withholding rent properly.

**Relevant Evidence:** Tenants have a right to withhold rent because landlords must provide safe habitable housing under this legal obligation that violates if bad conditions exist. If landlord breaks this duty by failing to fix known defects after notice and refusal to make repairs, tenant's rental payment responsibility stops until repairs occur (Legal Tactics Ch 8, pg 17).

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)

results.question: I want to break my lease early in Massachusetts. What are the consequences?
results.response:
## Relevant Evidence:
- If you move out because of serious violations and do not have a written agreement with your landlord that allows for an earlier termination, you must prove there were very bad conditions when you left. You will also need to show you had no other good options available at the time (MassLegalHelp.org/Breaking-Lease). Your landlord may still try to sue you for unpaid rent after you move out.
- If your lease has a clause saying that if you leave before it ends, you are responsible for paying any remaining balance of rent owed on your lease term when you quit, this can be difficult to get around. You will need to show the conditions were so bad they forced you into early termination (MassLegalHelp.org/Breaking-Lease).
- If violations are not serious or you do not have a Board of Health report showing unsafe/health hazards in the apartment that caused your move out, then if you leave without proper notice and payment for any remaining balance due on your lease term is owed to landlord at time when you quit (MassLegalHelp.org/Breaking-Lease).

## Analysis:
You can be held responsible by a court or contractually liable for unpaid rent after early termination. The law allows tenants with serious health/safety violations in their apartment the right to break leases and move out without being punished financially, but this only applies if there is an official Board of Health report documenting such conditions (MassLegalHelp.org/Breaking-Lease). You must follow specific legal steps including sending written notice that you are "surrendering" your lease back with a clear agreement from the landlord accepting early termination and returning keys on or before agreed upon date to avoid being held liable for rent owed after move out.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 11: Moving Out](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/11%20Moving%20Out%202025.pdf)

results.question: My landlord is trying to charge me a late fee after 5 days. Is this allowed in MA?
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
Landlords cannot collect interest or fees until rent payments are at least thirty (30) days behind, unless there's a written agreement that allows it before the third month of tenancy and with no grace period for payment.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- Late Fees in Public Housing prohibits charging late fees except through approved assistance programs.
- The landlord cannot collect any interest or penalty until at least 30 calendar days after rent is due. Even if there's a written agreement that allows it, the fee can only be charged once you are thirty (90) full calendar days behind on your payment of rent [Chapter Five: Rent].

---
**Sources:**

results.question: Can my landlord refuse to rent to me because I have kids? (Massachusetts)
results.response:
## Question Understanding: 
A person cannot discriminate against you in housing or employment because of your family status. This includes refusing a rental agreement due to having children under 18 years old.

## Relevant Evidence:
Families with children are protected from discrimination by the fair housing laws and Massachusetts law, which prohibits landlords who rent any kind of housing built before 1978 that has lead paint from discriminating against families because they have young children.23 It is illegal for a landlord to refuse to show you an apartment or offer you terms conditioned on your having children under six years old in violation of MGL c.54, §16B(2). The law prohibits refusing to rent due to lead paint as this would be considered indirect discrimination against families with young children who must live in a deleading process for the property and its conditions after they move in or have difficulty finding an apartment that is not "lead" painted based on their family status.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/09%20Lead%20Poisoning%202025.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Discrimination](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/07%20Discrimination%202025.pdf)
- [Sites Default Files File 2020 03 Renters English Lead 20Fact 20Sheet](https://www.boston.gov/sites/default/files/file/2020/03/Renters_English_Lead%20Fact%20Sheet.pdf)

results.question: My landlord changed the locks while I was away for a week. Is this an illegal eviction in MA?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** The inquiry is whether changing locks without court permission constitutes an illegal action under Massachusetts law that can be challenged during evictions and lockout prevention efforts.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- It's illegal to "substantially interfere" with a tenant’s use of their apartment through self-help tactics. This includes locking out tenants or changing the locks in any way without court permission (Legal Tactics Ch. 12 Evictions, p.345).
- A landlord may be violating both civil and criminal laws if they change your lock as part of an illegal "self-help" tactic to take away a tenant's apartment through intimidation tactics that violate quiet enjoyment rights.
- Landlords cannot use self-help tactics like changing locks without going to court first (Legal Tactics Ch. 12 Evictions, p.345).

---
**Sources:**

results.question: I reported code violations and now my landlord is trying to evict me. Is this retaliation? (MA)
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
Landlords may not retaliate against tenants for reporting suspected lead paint or other health code violations in a building, including through an official inspection process where the tenant was told about problems that needed fixing.

## Relevant Evidence: 
It is illegal to discriminate in housing because of familial status (having children under 6) and/or being pregnant. A landlord may not refuse to rent, lease, represent for sale or rental, make a major change in terms offered, raise your rents, evict you nor fail to correct known violations if the tenant has reported an inspection finding related to lead paint hazards where any child younger than six lives at that address [Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning].

results.question: What is the Section 8 housing program and how do I apply in Boston?
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
Section 8 Housing: A federal rental assistance program that helps pay a portion of rent to cover income limits. Applicants go through an application screening process where they are interviewed, checked for eligibility based on their household size/income/other criteria; and then placed onto one or more waiting lists depending upon the housing type(s) chosen by applicants during this initial application phase.
The Section 8 Housing program is comprised of two types: Tenant-based vouchers (Mobile Vouchers), which can be used to rent into any apartment meeting minimum standards, as well as Project-Based Voucher programs tied specifically with certain developments owned or managed by private companies.

---

results.question: Can my landlord charge me for bed bug treatment in Massachusetts?
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
A landlord may not illegally add fees or charges. A security deposit cannot be used to pay for pest control unless there is damage that caused it and you agreed. Landlords must fix lead paint problems when known; they can't refuse housing because of children under 6. You do NOT have the right to live in a lead-free home, but landlords who rent before 1978 with kids need to remove or cover all dangerous lead paint hazards (MGL c.235 s.4).

---

If your apartment complex is sold but you have a valid rental agreement that has not yet ended, it's illegal for either an old or new owner to raise your rent without proper notice. The terms of existing agreements are binding on any successor owners unless there’s good cause (e.g., foreclosure).

Landlords can't discriminate against tenants because of their need for or use of services,
accommodations, and/or auxiliary aids. A tenant cannot be denied housing based on the fact that they have children under six years old.
It is illegal to refuse to rent an apartment or make any other adverse action (such as increasing

results.question: My landlord is deducting my security deposit for things that were broken when I moved in. What can I do? (MA)
results.response:

You cannot be charged for damage or wear and tear beyond "reasonable" deterioration due to normal use, as determined by the state sanitary code at 105 CMR 427 - Minimum Standards of Fitness for Human Habitation. Landlords must separate security deposits from their regular bank accounts according to M.G.L c. 93A §8.A.

## Relevant Evidence:
Landlord cannot deduct reasonable wear and tear or damage already existing when you move in: "Your landlord can only use your deposit after you leave the apartment for repairs needed because of damages beyond 'reasonable wear and tear'." (Legal Tactics Ch. 5, Security Deposits at page 4)

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: Can I sue my landlord in small claims court for not returning my security deposit in MA?
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
You can file a case about your security deposit and ask up to $7000. If you win, but only if there is no damage or unpaid rent owed on the day of termination. You may be awarded 3 times what was not returned by the landlord in addition.

**Relevant Evidence:**
If you move out before a written lease ends your security deposit can still be held until after an additional thirty days from when your tenancy actually ended (not just the end date indicated on paper). If there is no damage or unpaid rent owed, it must return within 30 days of the day that you leave. You may ask for up to three times what was not returned in addition if they failed to properly deposit money into a separate bank account protected from creditors and did so illegally (Mass Legal Help, Chapter Three: Security Deposits).

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

A tenant's security deposit must be held by their landlord in an interest-bearing bank or trust account kept separately from the owner’s funds protected from creditors, as required under M.G.L., c 186 §20B(3). The right to hold a security deposit in separate and creditor-protected accounts is established for all tenancies created on or after July 9, 2017.

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston and my lease is for 12 months. What are my rights regarding the security deposit my landlord is holding?
results.response:
## Question Understanding:
Landlords can charge tenants a security deposit of up to one month's rent, but it must be separate from last month’s rent or first/last month's rent payments that landlords may also request under MGL c. 186 s.4A(3). Tenants are protected by the state law against illegal fees charged in addition to a security deposit and can only legally pay for reasonable repairs, unpaid real estate tax increases (if required), or damages not "reasonable wear and tear."

**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: I just found out my landlord is selling the building. What happens to my security deposit?
results.response:
## Question Understanding: 
If your apartment’s owner sells their property and you have a security deposit in an interest-bearing account with separate bank accounts for each tenant, then when they sell it that money must be transferred into a new holding company's checking or savings account. This transfer has to happen within 30 days after the sale of the building. If your landlord fails to follow this rule about transferring funds upon closing and sells their property without doing so you have an immediate right in court for triple damages with interest owed on that security deposit as well as attorney’s fees paid by them, per MGL c.186 s 24A(3)(b) & (c).

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: Can my landlord in Boston legally require me to pay for the cost of repairing normal wear and tear when I move out?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** The user is asking whether their apartment lease allows them to be charged a fee for repairs that are considered part of "reasonable" or "normal wear and tear." This would include things like peeling paint, worn carpeting, broken lightbulbs in fixtures, etc. They want to know if they can legally exclude this cost from the security deposit calculation as required by Massachusetts law.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- A landlord may not use a tenant's security deposit for "reasonable wear and tear" repairs (Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits). Landlord must pay for damages beyond normal wear & tear only.
- G.L. c.186, s.27B prohibits landlords from requiring tenants to repair ordinary or reasonable wear and tear of the apartment as part of a security deposit obligation.
- The tenant is responsible for any damage that exceeds "reasonable" or “ordinary” wear and tear (Attorney General's Guide).
- A landlord may use your security deposit only to pay for repairs needed because you didn't follow through on repairing things damaged by yourself, guests, etc. Landlord cannot use it for reasonable wear & tear issues like peeling paint in the hallways that were there when they started renting us this apartment (Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits).

---
**Sources:**
- [Attorney General's Guide to Landlord and Tenant Rights](https://www.mass.gov/guides/the-attorney-generals-guide-to-landlord-and-tenant-rights)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)
- [Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits](https://www.mass.gov/info-details/massachusetts-law-about-tenants-security-deposits)

results.question: Can my landlord in Boston legally turn off my heat during the winter if I'm late on rent?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** The tenant asks whether their landlord can shut off heating services as a penalty for being behind on rent. This relates to housing conditions and eviction protections under Massachusetts law.

**Relevant Evidence:**
- Landlords must provide heat from September 15 through May 31 per MGL c.284, §9A.
- A landlord cannot raise or shut off your rent if you have a lease without proper notice (M.G.L., Chapter 76/Section 2).
- If the heating goes out and tenant calls their landlord immediately but they don't fix it within reasonable time then Tenant can call local Board of Health who must inspect home to see what conditions violate state Sanitary Code.
- Landlord cannot evict you, raise your rent or make a major change in rental agreement because you asked for repairs (M.G.L. c186 s2).
- If landlord refuses/fails to fix problems after notice and the tenant calls city's Board of Health then an inspector will examine home as soon as possible if there are violations that violate state Sanitary Code.

**Analysis:** Landlord cannot shut off heat during required heating season without violating M.G.L. c.186 s2 which says it is illegal for a landlord to evict you, raise your rent or make major changes in rental agreement because tenant asked about bad conditions including no heat (MGL 40P §3A). Landlord must fix problems with habitable space within reasonable time after notice of need. If they refuse/ignore needs then Tenant can call local Board of Health who has authority to inspect and order repairs before evicting or changing rent terms in agreement.

**Final Answer:** It is illegal for a landlord to turn off your heating as punishment if you're late on rent payments according to MGL c186 s2. Landlord must fix problems with habitable space within reasonable time after notice of need violations, and tenant can call local Board of Health who has authority under state law to inspect before evicting or changing terms in rental agreement due to bad conditions including no heat.

---

results.question: Can I withhold rent if my apartment in Boston has serious code violations?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** You are asking whether you can legally take back some of your rent for bad conditions. This is called "rent withholding." The law says tenants have a right to withhold when there are unsafe or unhealthy problems that the landlord knew about and did not fix, as long as certain other things line up in this specific way.

**Relevant Evidence:**
You can legally withhold if you answer yes (or “agree”) to all five of these questions below:
1. Do defective conditions exist?
2. Endanger/Impair health/safety/well-being? See the Housing Code Checklist.
3. Did landlord know about problem before your rent was behind and did they ignore it?
4. Were bad conditions caused by something other than you, guest or household member under your control (e.g., a leaking roof)?
5. Can landlord make repairs without permanent moving out?

If you answer yes to all five questions above: You can legally withhold the amount of money that is needed in order for you and any co-tenants who agreed on withholding rent not only to pay, but also have an adequate buffer so they don’t go behind on their own or other people’s future payments. See Chapter 3: Security Deposits & Last Month's Rent at www.masslegalhelp.org/Chapter03.htmland the booklets of this chapter (Booklet #1-4). If you and your co-tenants agreed to withhold, then each person is responsible for paying back only their own portion of any money they withheld. See Chapter 5: Co-Tenancy at www.masslegalhelp.org/Chapter06.htmland the booklets from this chapter (Booklet #1 & Booklet #2). If you do not have a written agreement with your co-tenants, then each person is responsible for paying back all of any money they withheld. See Chapter 5: Co-Tenancy at www.masslegalhelp.org/Chapter06.htmland the booklets from this chapter (Booklet #1 & Booklet #2).

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)

results.question: My apartment in Boston has been unbearably hot this winter, constantly over 78°F. Is my landlord violating any regulations?
results.response:
**Question Understanding:** The user is asking whether their apartment's excessive heat constitutes a violation of Massachusetts law or housing codes.

**Relevant Evidence:**
Landlords are required to provide appropriate heating so that the temperature does not exceed "a reasonable amount" in winter. Excessive noise, vibration and other disturbances violate quiet enjoyment rights if they interfere with peaceful occupancy (Legal Tactics Ch. 13). Landlord interference includes failure or refusal of access necessary for repair work when a tenant has notified them about dangerous conditions before moving out (Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made; Legal Tactics Ch. 25, s.6-7).

**Analysis:** The Massachusetts Sanitary Code sets minimum standards for housing including heating requirements in MGL c.140B §9(3). Landlords are prohibited from transferring to tenants the responsibility of paying utility bills that are their own obligation (Legal tactics ch.8 - Breach Quiet Enjoyment or Interference with Utilities, p25-6; Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Tenants' Rights in Massachusetts pp140-9)

---
**Sources:**

results.question: Can my landlord block the hallway with furniture in our apartment building in Boston?
results.response:
## Question Understanding: 
Tenants have a right against having their means of egress blocked. This includes hallways where residents may need to pass through at all hours and during emergencies.

**Relevant Evidence:** The Sanitary Code prohibits blocking any way that is necessary for the use or enjoyment of an apartment, including by furniture in common areas (105 CMR 430: Landlord-Tenant Relationships). It also requires means-of-egress to be free from obstruction.

---
**Sources:**
